coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocatalytic H2-production activity of C-dots modified g-C3N4/TiO2 nanosheets composites
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yang
Feng, Xionghan
Lu, Zhexue
Yin, Hui
Liu, Fan
Xiang, Quanjun
coredata.dc:description: As a new carbon-based material, carbon dots (C-dots) have got widely preference because of its excellent electronic transfer capability. In this work, a novel ternary layered C-dots/g-C3N4/TiO2 nanosheets (CGT) composite photocatalysts were prepared by impregnation precipitation methods. The optimal ternary CGT composite samples revealed high photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate in triethanolamine aqueous solutions, which exceeded the rate of the optimal g-C3N4/TiO2 composite sample by a factor of 5 times. The improved photocatalytic activity is owed to the positive effects of C-dots and layered heterojunction structure of TiO2 nanosheets and g-C3N4 sheets. C-dots in the CGT composites can serve as electron reservoirs to capture the photo-induced electrons. The well-defined layered heterojunction structure of CGT provides the intimate contact and the strong interaction of anatase TiO2 nanosheets and g-C3N4 sheets via face-to-face orientation, which restrains the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers, and thus enhances the photocatalytic H2-production activity. Electron paramagnetic resonance and transient photocurrent response proved the strong interaction and improved interfacial charge transfer of TiO2 nanosheets and g-C3N4 sheets, respectively. The mechanism of improving the photocatalytic H2-evolution activity was further confirmed by time-resolved fluorescence, electron paramagnetic resonance, transient photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Graphitic carbon nitride
Photocatalytic H2 production
TiO2 nanosheets
Face-to-face

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and Raman spectra

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 51672099 and 21403079 ) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2662015PY039 , 2662015PY210 and 2662017JC017 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (A) the as-prepared ternary CGT1, CGT2, CGT3 samples and (B) the as-prepared GT, CT, CG, CGT samples.
TEM images of the as-prepared (a) C-dots, (b, c) CGT2, (d) CT, (e) CG and (f) GT samples.
(a, b) SEM image of CGT2; (c) multi-elemental content of C, O, N and Ti; (d) multi-elemental map of C, O, N and Ti; (e) C element map; (f) N element map; (g) O element map; (h) Ti element map.
UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of (A) the as-prepared TiO2, CGT1, CGT2, CGT3 and g-C3N4, and (B) the as-prepared TiO2, GT, CG, CT, CGT2 and g-C3N4.
(A) XPS survey spectrum of the CGT2 sample and high-resolution XPS spectra of (B) C 1s, (C) N 1s and (D) Ti 2p for the CGT2 sample.
(A) FTIR spectra of the TiO2, CGT2, CG and g-C3N4 samples and (B) Raman spectra of the CT, C-dots, CGT2 and CG samples. Inset: Raman spectra of the samples from 1250 cm−1 to 1750 cm−1.
Hydrogen evolution rate (HER) of (A) the as-prepared ternary CGT1, CGT2, CGT3 samples and (B) the as-prepared CGT2, CT, GT, CG samples.
Proposed photocatalytic mechanism for CGT composite photocatalysts.
EPR spectra of the g-C3N4, GT, and CGT2 samples.
Time-resolved fluorescence spectra of the g-C3N4, GT, CG and CGT2 samples.
Recycling experiment of the CGT2 and GT samples in HER.
(A) The transient photocurrent responses of CG, GT, CT and CGT2 samples and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of (B) CG, GT, CT and CGT2.
The detailed information and substrate of the synthesized samples.
The fluorescence decay lifetimes and their percentages of photo-induced carriers in the pristine g-C3N4, GT, CG and CGT2 composites.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocatalytic H2-production activity of C-dots modified g-C3N4/TiO2 nanosheets composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Resources and Environment, Huazhong Agricultural University, Wuhan 430070, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Huazhong Agricultural University, Wuhan 430070, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The heterojunction structure of TiO2 nanosheets and g-C3N4 sheets and unique electronic transfer capability of the C-dots enhanced the photocatalyticH2-production activity of the ternary layered C-dots/g-C3N4/TiO2 nanosheets (CGT) composite photocatalysts.

As a new carbon-based material, carbon dots (C-dots) have got widely preference because of its excellent electronic transfer capability. In this work, a novel ternary layered C-dots/g-C3N4/TiO2 nanosheets (CGT) composite photocatalysts were prepared by impregnation precipitation methods. The optimal ternary CGT composite samples revealed high photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate in triethanolamine aqueous solutions, which exceeded the rate of the optimal g-C3N4/TiO2 composite sample by a factor of 5 times. The improved photocatalytic activity is owed to the positive effects of C-dots and layered heterojunction structure of TiO2 nanosheets and g-C3N4 sheets. C-dots in the CGT composites can serve as electron reservoirs to capture the photo-induced electrons. The well-defined layered heterojunction structure of CGT provides the intimate contact and the strong interaction of anatase TiO2 nanosheets and g-C3N4 sheets via face-to-face orientation, which restrains the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers, and thus enhances the photocatalytic H2-production activity. Electron paramagnetic resonance and transient photocurrent response proved the strong interaction and improved interfacial charge transfer of TiO2 nanosheets and g-C3N4 sheets, respectively. The mechanism of improving the photocatalytic H2-evolution activity was further confirmed by time-resolved fluorescence, electron paramagnetic resonance, transient photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:para: Facing the environment problem and energy crisis, the development of new energy has attracted growing attention. Among various new energies, hydrogen energy is one of the most promising alternative fuels in the future for its high energy capacity and environment friendly. At present, the methods of preparing hydrogen energy are mainly coal gasification and electrolysis [1,2]. However, the high temperature and high energy consumption limit the application of the aforementioned two methods. Therefore, many researchers have focused their attention on the development of the potential technology for the aim of efficiently yielding hydrogen under the normal pressure and temperature [3]. Photocatalytic hydrogen production has attracted growing attention since Fujishima and Honda reported the use of TiO2 electrode to split water [4]. As a n-type semiconductor, TiO2 has long been recognized as one of the most ideal photocatalysts because of suitable band gap of about 3.2 eV and its chemical inertness and optical property, low cost, environment friendly [5–7]. However, the photocatalytic ability of TiO2 is restricted due to the rapid recombination of the photogeneration charges [8]. Many strategies have been proposed to improve the photocatalytic activity of TiO2. For instance, modifying co-catalysts (rGo [9], MoS2 [10]), changing its shape (such as nanosheets [11], nanotube [12]), coupling other photocatalysts (CdS [13], WS2 [14], some metal oxides [15–17]), and calcining [18]. In particular, it is greatly interesting to couple TiO2 with CdS, which formed the heterojunction to improve the photocatalytic performance. Unfortunately, the CdS photocatalyst is prone to light corrosion and produce toxic cadmium [19]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to find other photocatalytic materials instead of CdS.

Recently, more and more attention has been paid on the non-metal photocatalysts due to their chemical stability and cost effectiveness. The layered non-metal graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) material was reported to be the potential photocatalyst owing to its chemical inertness and suitable band structure [20]. As a polymeric semiconductor, g-C3N4 sheets are of visible-light-responsive for better use of the solar spectrum than other photocatalysts of wide band-gap due to its narrow band gap energy of about 2.7 eV [21–23]. Moreover, the heterojunctions structure between g-C3N4 and other photocatalysts can benefit the transport of photogenerated electrons [24]. Considering the advantages of g-C3N4 sheets, it was expected that the introduction of g-C3N4 for modifying TiO2 observably enhanced the photocatalytic activity. In addition, it was worth noting that the face-to-face orientation of TiO2 nanosheets and g-C3N4 sheets significantly increased their contact area and reduced the accumulation of TiO2 nanosheets. Another non-metal material, C-dots as a new carbon-based material, are mainly used in biosensing [25], drug delivery [26,27], bioimaging [28]. Recently, C-dots are increasingly used in the field of photocatalysis because of their low cost, unique electronic transfer, and non-toxicity [29–31]. As a semiconductor, C-dots can keep up on the excited state for long time due to its structural characteristics [32,33]. This feature is good for trapping photo-induced electrons which come from the conduct band (CB) of the photocatalysts for C-dots-modified other photocatalysts.

Here, we report a series of novel ternary layered C-dots/g-C3N4/TiO2 nanosheets (CGT) composite photocatalysts for photocatalytic H2 production. The CGT composite photocatalysts were prepared by impregnation precipitation methods. To get a better understanding of the photocatalytic mechanism of the CGT photocatalysts, the binary photocatalysts concluding C-dots/g-C3N4 (CG), C-dots/TiO2 nanosheets (CT), g-C3N4/TiO2 nanosheets (GT) were also synthesized by the similar method as that of CGT photocatalysts. The mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic H2-production activity of the CGT photocatalysts was proposed based on the time-resolved fluorescence, electron paramagnetic resonance, transient photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy.
Tetrabutyltitanate (TBT), hydrofluoric acid (40 wt%), ammonia (30 wt%), melamine and urea were analytical grade. Distilled water was used in all experiments.

C-dots were synthesized on the basis of the literature but modified as follow [34]: Firstly, a glass plate was put on the top of burning candles to collect the candle soot. Secondly, the collected soot was scattered in 50 ml of ammonia solution by ultrasonicating for 30 min. Then, the obtained suspensions were transferred from beaker to Teflon-lined autoclave and hydrothermal treatment for 7 h at 150 °C in an oven. Finally, after washed with water and ethanol, the precipitates were dried in the oven at 80 °C.

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) sheets were synthesized through calcining the mixture of urea and melamine. Specific steps are as follows, 20 mmol (1.2 g) urea and 20 mmol (2.5 g) melamine were mixed in crucible at 550 °C for 4 h with a heating rate of 5 °C/min in an oven. The obtained yellow precipitates milled into powers and gathered to further use after cooling down to the room temperature.

TiO2 nanosheets were prepared through a primitive hydrothermal treatment method on the basis of the literature [35]. In a typical preparation experiment, 25 ml of tetrabutyltitanate (TBT) and 3 ml of hydrofluoric acid (40 wt%) were mixed in Teflon-lined autoclave and hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C and kept for 24 h after magnetic stirring. It is worth noting that the obtained suspensions should wash with NaOH aqueous solution firstly for removing the fluorinion on the surface of TiO2. The obtained precipitate was exsiccated at 80 °C and kept for 6 h in an oven after washed with water and ethanol.

C-dots/g-C3N4/TiO2 (CGT) ternary samples were synthesized through a facile impregnation precipitation method. In a typical synthesis of CGT1, 0.01 g of C-dots, 0.2 g of g-C3N4 and 0.2 g of TiO2 were mixed in a beaker with 50 ml of deionized water and static settlement for 9 h after magnetic stirring for 2 h. After the centrifugation washed with water and ethanol, the obtained precipitate was dried at 60 °C and kept for 10 h in an oven. The nominal weight ratios of g-C3N4 to TiO2 was designated as 1:1, 1:2 and 2:1, the obtained samples were corresponding to CGT1, CGT2, CGT3, and the content of C-dots is invariable. The other binary materials including CG (C-dots/g-C3N4), CT (C-dots/TiO2), GT (g-C3N4/TiO2) were synthesized through the similar method as that of CGT1, but the weight of C-dots,g-C3N4 and TiO2 changed to be 0.01 g, 0.2 g and 0.4 g, respectively. The detailed amount of the sample used in the synthesis process is shown in Table 1 .

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was recorded on a D8-Advance diffractometer (Bruker, German) by using Ni-filtered Cu Kα radiation at a scan rate (2θ) of 10° min−1 with accelerating voltage of 40 kV and current of 40 mA. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was recorded on Hitachi H-7650 (HITACHI, Japan) transmission electron microscope. The acceleration voltage was set as 200 kV. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) analysis was taken on a JEM-2100F (JEOL, Japan) microscope. The acceleration voltage was set as 200 kV. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy were conducted on S-4800 (HITACHI, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectrum (UV–Vis DRS) spectra were obtained on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 650s), Teflon was used as reflectance standard. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was detected on a Leybold Heraeus-Shenyang SKL-12 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with Mg Kα as excitation source. The accelerating voltage and applied current were 10 kV and 15 mA, respectively. The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded by using an IRAffinity-1 FTIR spectrometer. Raman spectra were taken at room temperature under ambient conditions on a micro-Raman spectrometer (Renishaw InVia). The excitation source is a 633 nm Ar + laser. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were conducted on an EPR spectrometer (Bruker, German) at room temperature. The time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra were detected at room temperature on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, FS5).

The photocatalytic H2-production experiments of the prepared photocatalysts were measured in a 100 ml three openings flask at atmospheric pressure and room temperature. The silicone rubber septum was used to seal the flask. A 350 W Xe arc lamp as the light source, and the lamp was put 20 cm over the photocatalytic reactor with the ca. 70 mW/cm2 focused intensity on the flask. Specific steps are as follows, 50 mg of composite materials were dispersed in 80 ml of aqueous solution containing 10% of triethanolamine scavenger in volume. After the mixture suspension solution of photocatalysts and sacrificial agent was degassed using N2 for 30 min to dislodge the remainder oxygen and air in the flask. After a 60 min of illumination with continuous magnetic stirring, 0.4 ml gas was collected to analyze on a gas chromatograph (GC-2004C, Japan). The apparent quantum efficiency (QE) was conducted under the similar method as the photocatalytic H2-production experiments, only the light source was taken the place of 2 low-power LED lamps (3 W, 365 nm), and the two LED lamps were put 1 cm over the flask in the opposite sides. The illumination area and focused intensity of each UV-LED were 1 cm2 and ca. 80 mW/cm2 on the flask, respectively. The QE was carried out by the nether equation:
QE
[ % ] =
number of reacted electrons
number of incident photons
× 100
=
number of evolved H
2
molecules × 2
number of incident photons
× 100

Photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were measured on a CHI660E (Shanghai Chenhua Limited, China) electrochemical analyzer. The analyzer was a three-electrode system which using the Pt wire and Ag/AgCl as the counter electrode and reference electrode, the working electrodes was the prepared glass electrodes with an effectively area of ca. 1.2 cm2. A 350 W Xe arc lamp and 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution were used as the light source and the electrolyte, respectively.

The glass working electrode was synthesized as follow: the mixture of 0.05 g of the sample, 0.05 g of polyethyleneglycol (PEG) and 0.5 ml of ethanol was ground into a slurry. Then the use of the doctor blade method to the slurry evenly coated onto a 2 cm × 1.2 cm F-doped SnO2-coated (FTO) glass electrode [36,37]. Finally, the obtained glass electrodes were dried at 100 °C for 60 min in an oven.

Fig. 1 A shows the XRD patterns of CGT1, CGT2, CGT3 samples. It can be seen that the characteristic diffraction peak positions are located at 2θ = 25.4, 37.8, 48.1, 54.0, 62.8, 68.9, 75.2 and 26.5°, corresponding to (1 0 1), (0 0 4), (2 0 0), (1 0 5), (2 0 4), (1 1 6), (2 1 5) crystal plane of anatase and (0 0 2) crystal face of g-C3N4 [38,39], respectively. No characteristic peaks of C-dots were found due to its low loading content and the weak crystallization. Further observation shows when the ratio of g-C3N4 to TiO2 changes from 1:1 (CGT1) to 1:2 (CGT2), the characteristic peak intensity of g-C3N4 is obviously increased. Changing the ratio to 2:1 (CGT3), the characteristic peaks intensity of g-C3N4 reached the maximum. This result shows that the characteristic peaks intensity of g-C3N4 will be enhanced with increasing the content of g-C3N4, indicating a good dispersion of the CGT photocatalytic materials. Fig. 1B shows the XRD patterns of the binary samples included CT, CG, GT and CGT. As we can see, all the characteristic peaks of TiO2 and g-C3N4 are detected and there are no significant change in the position of the peak in the aforementioned two figures. These results suggest that the binary and ternary composite photocatalysts were successfully prepared.

The C-dots were synthesized by utilizing the soot which produced by the incomplete combustion of candle. As Fig. 2 a shown, the size of C-dots are mostly in diameter of 10–20 nm with lattice spacing is ca. 0.20 nm (Fig. 3 a and b), according with the requirement of C-dots [40,41]. The TEM images of CGT2 are shown in Fig. 2b. As shown in figure, TiO2 nanosheets were deposited on the large sheet of g-C3N4, a small amount of C-dots mixed in the TiO2 and g-C3N4 junction (Fig. 2c). Further observation shows that the g-C3N4 consisted of 2D irregular sheet. Whereas TiO2 is rectangular nanosheets with length of 80 nm, width of 40 nm and the lattice spacing is ca. 0.35 nm (Fig. 3b and d), corresponding to (1 0 1) lattice plane of anatase [42]. The TEM images of CT and CG samples are shown in Fig. 2d and e. The C-dots is sporadically distribution on the g-C3N4 sheets and TiO2 nanosheets, meaning that C-dots have been successfully modified on TiO2 nanosheets and g-C3N4 sheets. Fig. 2f shows the TEM images of the GT composites. As shown in figure, the TiO2 nanosheets deposited on the large sheets of g-C3N4. This method of the face-to-face contact can increase the contact area of g-C3N4 sheets and TiO2 nanosheets, and thus accelerate the transfer of photoinduced electrons. Fig. 4 a and b shows the SEM images of the CGT2 samples. It can be clearly exhibited that the TiO2 nanosheets were deposited on the bulk g-C3N4, but the C-dots was not observed in the Fig. 4b because of its small diameter and the interference of other substances. The composition and distribution of the elements in the CGT2 samples is measured by energy dispersive X-ray spectrum (EDX). Fig. 4b shows the scanning surface image of EDX. As shown in Fig. 4c and d, the signals of C, N, Ti and O elements are detected. The C element is filled with the entire scanning surface and the distribution of N element matches with that of C element (Fig. 4e and f), indicating the presence of g-C3N4. Meanwhile, the O element distribution is also according to that of Ti element (Fig. 4 g and h). This result further proves the existence of g-C3N4 and TiO2 in CGT2 photocatalyst.

Fig. 5 A shows the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of TiO2, CGT1, CGT2, CGT3, and g-C3N4. It can be seen that the curve of pure TiO2 has a significantly increase at wavelengths of 380 nm, agreeing with the intrinsic absorption edge of anatase TiO2 [8]. The pure g-C3N4 curve also shows a significant increase at wavelengths of 460 nm, corresponding to its bandgap of 2.7 eV. All the ternary CGTx (x = 1, 2 and 3) composite materials have similar spectra, which exhibit two absorption steps. The first step is from 200 to 400 nm, corresponding to the intrinsic absorption of pure TiO2. The second step is form 400 to 500 nm, corresponding to the intrinsic absorption of pure g-C3N4. This fact indicates the existence of g-C3N4 and TiO2 in the CGTx photocatalysts. In addition, all the ternary CGTx samples exhibit strong background absorption. This is due to the presence of the black C-dots. The optical absorption of the black C-dots can increase the background absorption. This result also can be confirmed by UV–Vis DRS spectra of primitive TiO2, the binary GT, CT and CG composites (Fig. 5B). It can be seen that the CT and CG curve display strong background absorption with the introduction of C-dots. However, the GT composite materials only show two absorption steps instead of the increased background absorption. So the increased background absorption should be attributable to the optical absorption of the black C-dots. Moreover, all the absorption edge of the CGTx samples show a blue shift in comparison with pure g-C3N4 and TiO2. The enlarged band gap can be owe to the layered heterojunction structure of TiO2 nanosheets and g-C3N4 sheets.

The chemical composition and status of C, N, Ti and O elements in the CGTx composites were further analyzed by XPS analysis. It can be seen from Fig. 6 A that the photoelectron peaks located at binding energies of 285 eV (C 1 s), 399 eV (N 1 s), 458 eV (Ti 2p) and 530 eV (O 1 s), corresponding to the survey spectrum of the C, N, Ti and O in the CGT2 sample, respectively.

Fig. 6B shows that the two peaks located at 284.6 eV and 288.2 eV were observed in the C 1 s spectrum. The former peak at 284.6 eV is corresponding to a standard specialized for carbon in the CGT2 composites, and the latter one at 288.2 eV is the sp2-hybridized carbon which is ascribed to NCN2 coordination of the g-C3N4 [43–45]. The high resolution N 1s curves (Fig. 6C) could be observed three peaks at 398.5, 400.3 and 404.3 eV, which were assigned to pyridinic, pyrrolic and graphitic N species, respectively [44]. From the high resolution Ti 2p spectrum of CGT2 (Fig. 6D), the peak of Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2 were observed at 464.5 and 458.8 eV, which were corresponding to the binding energy of Ti4+ in TiO2 [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and Raman spectra
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectra of g-C3N4, CG, CGT2 and TiO2 samples are shown in Fig. 7 A. In the curve of pure TiO2, there are three peaks in region of 400–700, 1631, and 3480 cm−1, and the peak at 400–700 cm−1 is the main peaks, corresponding to Ti-O stretching and Ti-O-Ti bridging stretching modes. The peak at 1631 cm−1 is attributed to the surface-adsorbed water, while the last peak at 3480 cm−1 is assigned to hydroxyl groups [47]. For g-C3N4, the main peaks arising at 1200–1650 cm−1, with the peaks region of 1241, 1322, 1406, 1571, and 1631 cm−1, is attributed to the typical stretching modes of CN heterocycles [48]. Additionally, the peak at 806 cm−1 was observed, corresponding to breathing mode of triazine units [39,44]. All the main characteristic peaks of functional group in the g-C3N4 and TiO2 were observed in the all samples. However, no characteristic peaks of C-dots were found in the spectra due to the low intensity. The existence of C-dots in the prepared CGTx samples can be proved by the Raman spectra. It can be seen from Fig. 7B that the curve of C-dots appeared two prominent peaks located at about 1335 and 1607 cm−1, which is assigned to the D and G band of carbon [49]. Also, the two characteristic peaks of C-dots are also appeared in CT samples, meaning that the C-dots have been succeed in modifying on TiO2 nanosheets. In the CG and CGT2 samples, only one peaks at 1607 cm−1 is displayed, and the peaks at 1335 cm−1 is masked by the characteristic peak of g-C3N4. These results confirm the presence of C-dots in the CGTx photocatalysts.

The photocatalytic activity was reflected by the hydrogen evolution rate (HER) in the existence of triethanolamine. Fig. 8 A shows the HER of CGT1, CGT2 and CGT3. It’s clearly observed that the HER is influenced by the weight ratios of g-C3N4 to TiO2 in the CGTx samples. When the ratio was 1:2 (CGT2), the HER reached the highest value with an apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of 3.5%. Reducing the content of TiO2 or increasing the amount of g-C3N4 to the ratio of g-C3N4 to TiO2 of 1:1 (CGT1) or 2:1 (CGT3), the HER was decreased comparing with that of CGT2. This is because that the reduced TiO2 or increased the amount of g-C3N4 led a decrease distribution of TiO2 nanosheets on the g-C3N4 sheets. This result suggests that the formation of the ternary CGT system was decreased due to the content invariably of C-dots. Fig. 8B shows the HER of the CGT2, CT, GT, CG samples. The CT samples showed relatively good photocatalytic H2-production compared with that of the GT composites. This is probably caused by the ability of C-dots to transfer electrons which came from the conduction band (CB) of TiO2 were stronger than g-C3N4 in the CGTx photocatalytic system. Meanwhile, the HER of GT samples was higher than that of CG samples. This suggests that the ability of TiO2 to transfer electrons was stronger than that of g-C3N4 in the CGTx photocatalytic system.

Combined with the aforementioned characterization and analysis, the tentative mechanism for the photocatalytic H2 production over CT and CG samples is proposed in Fig. 9 . Under irradiation of the Xe lamp, C-dots are regarded as electron cisterns to capture photogenerated electrons from the CB of TiO2 or g-C3N4, which retards the recombination of the photo-induced electron-hole pairs, and so that more photogenerated electrons participate in photocatalytic reactions, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic activity. For GT catalyst, due to the presence of heterojunctions structure between TiO2 nanosheets and g-C3N4 sheets, the photogenerated electrons in the CB of the g-C3N4 sheets are easily transferred to the more negative CB of the TiO2 nanosheets.

Based on the proposed mechanism of the binary photocatalysts, Fig. 9 also shows a tentative mechanism of the ternary C-dots/g-C3N4/TiO2 composites. Under irradiation, the TiO2 and g-C3N4 were excited to produce photo-induced electrons in the valence band (VB) of TiO2 and g-C3N4, which transferred to the CB of TiO2 and g-C3N4, respectively. Firstly, the presence of heterojunctions is beneficial for the transfer of electrons from the CB of g-C3N4 to that of TiO2. Secondly, C-dots can as an electron reservoir to arrest the photo-induced electrons from the CB of TiO2 and further retards the recombination of photogenerated carriers owing to the charge-storing advantage of C-dots [50,51].

The interfacial interaction of CGT composites system was measured using the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra. As shown in Fig. 10 , it’s obviously observed a significantly signal at 3513 G (g = 2.002) from the curve of g-C3N4, GT and CGT2 composites, corresponding to an unpaired electron on the carbon atoms of the aromatic rings within π-bonded nanosized clusters [52]. It is worth noting the intensity is enhanced distinctly after C-dots are coupled with GT, indicating the higher concentration of unpaired electrons for CGT2, which is very useful for the catalytic reaction.

To further verify the interaction of the three constituent in the ternary C-dots/g-C3N4/TiO2 composite, the lifetime of photoinduced carriers was measured by the time-resolved fluorescence spectra. The time-resolved fluorescence spectra of the g-C3N4, GT, CG and CGT2 samples were shown in Fig. 11 . The lifetimes and its percentage contribution of the fitting decay spectra are listed in Table 2 . It can be seen from Table 2 that the short life time of charge carriers in the CGTx composites was about 2.00 ns with the 43% of percentage contribution. All the long life time and its percentage contribution of photogenerated carriers were about 8.0 ns and 56%. The result of calculation showed that the average lifetime was about 5.30 ns.

The stability of the photocatalysts was analyzed by performing 4 circulation photocatalytic reactions of the CGT2 and GT samples (Fig. 12 ). The result shows that the HER of the CGT2 sample does not exhibit any obvious deactivation after 12 h illumination, indicating the strong stability of the CGT2 composites for photocatalytic H2 production.

The electrochemical experiments were used to further investigate the photogenerated carrier transfer and separation behaviors. It can be seen from Fig. 13 A that the CGT2 sample showed the highest photocurrent intensity, followed by CT, GT and CG samples. This result was matched with that of photocatalytic H2-production activity. It is interesting to note that the GT samples presented the minimum impedance in the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy spectra (Fig. 13B), indicated that the electrons were easy to transfer on the GT samples. However, the rapid recombination of photo-induced carriers restricted its photocatalytic activity. So the addition of the C-dots further improves the photocatalytic activity of the CGT composite photocatalysts through reducing the recombination of photogenerated carriers. For the CT and CGT2 samples, they show the higher impedance than the GT samples even though the CT and CGT2 samples present the higher photocatalytic H2-production than the GT sample. This is owing to the strong self-impedance of C-dots in the CT and CGT2 samples. Compared the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy spectra of the CT sample and CGT2 sample, the addition of g-C3N4 can reduce the impedance of the CGT2 sample, indicating an efficient transfer of the photogenerated carries in the presence of g-C3N4 for the CGT2 sample. This result indirectly suggests the key role of the heterojunction structure between g-C3N4 sheet and TiO2 nanosheets.

In summary, the ternary C-dots/g-C3N4/TiO2 composite photocatalysts were successfully prepared by impregnation precipitation method. The enhancement of photocatalytic H2-production activity was achieved by CGT photocatalysts in the photocatalytic reaction system used triethanolamine as sacrificing agent. The enhanced activity was because that the heterojunction of g-C3N4/TiO2 in the ternary C-dots/g-C3N4/TiO2 composites accelerated the transfer of the photogenerated electrons from the CB of g-C3N4 sheets to that of TiO2 nanosheets. In particular, the enlarged contact area and the strong interfacial interaction further enhanced the rate of electron transport in this process due to the contact of g-C3N4 sheets and TiO2 nanosheets via face-to-face orientation. Moreover, C-dots as electron reservoirs in the C-dots/g-C3N4/TiO2 composites captured the photogenerated electrons, facilitated the separation of photogenerated charge carriers, and thus enhanced the photocatalytic H2-production activity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 51672099 and 21403079) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2662015PY039, 2662015PY210 and 2662017JC017).
